subtopic_detail: Native valve infective endocarditis - empiric therapy
stem: A 58-year-old man with a history of degenerative aortic valve disease presents with 10 days of fevers, malaise, weight loss and a new exertional breathlessness. Examination reveals a soft pansystolic murmur not previously documented, splinter haemorrhages in two fingernails and microscopic haematuria on dipstick. Three sets of blood cultures are drawn from separate sites before any antibiotic is given. He has no penicillin allergy and is haemodynamically stable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 96, BP 118/64, RR 18, SpO2 96 percent room air
data_table.Examination: New apical pansystolic murmur, two splinter haemorrhages, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: WCC 14.2, CRP 142, creatinine 96, urinalysis 2+ blood 1+ protein, ECG sinus tachycardia, transthoracic echo pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empiric intravenous antibiotic regimen while cultures incubate?

options.text: Intravenous ceftriaxone 2 g daily as single-agent empiric therapy for community-acquired endocarditis
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone monotherapy does not cover staphylococci or enterococci adequately for native valve endocarditis empiric therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous benzylpenicillin 1.8 g every 4 hours plus flucloxacillin 2 g every 4 hours plus gentamicin 1 mg/kg every 8 hours
options.rationale: Triple therapy with benzylpenicillin, flucloxacillin and low-dose gentamicin gives appropriate coverage of streptococci, methicillin-susceptible staphylococci and enterococci pending culture and susceptibility results. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous vancomycin 25 mg/kg loading dose alone, withholding any beta-lactam until culture identification is available
options.rationale: Vancomycin monotherapy delays optimal beta-lactam therapy for susceptible organisms and is reserved for severe beta-lactam allergy or suspected methicillin-resistant infection. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Oral amoxicillin 1 g every 8 hours pending culture results given clinical stability and the prolonged subacute history
options.rationale: Endocarditis requires intravenous therapy regardless of clinical stability; oral therapy cannot achieve adequate bactericidal valve concentrations. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g every 6 hours as broad spectrum gram-negative cover for the subacute fever syndrome
options.rationale: Piperacillin-tazobactam targets gram-negative and anaerobic pathogens that are not the primary cause of native valve endocarditis. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Endocarditis)

explanation.summary: Empiric therapy for native valve endocarditis covers viridans streptococci, Staphylococcus aureus and enterococci because these account for the majority of cases. Combination therapy of benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin is the Australian guideline regimen pending culture identification. Three separate sets of blood cultures from different venepuncture sites before antibiotics are essential to meet the Duke microbiological criterion. Modified Duke criteria combine clinical, microbiological and echocardiographic findings to support diagnosis. Streamlining of therapy occurs once organism and susceptibilities are known.
key_points: Three separate blood culture sets before antibiotics.
Cover staph, strep and enterococci empirically.
Benzylpenicillin plus flucloxacillin plus gentamicin in Australia.
Vancomycin replaces beta-lactams only for severe allergy or MRSA suspicion.
Streamline once organism and MIC are known.
explanation.pearls: Gentamicin 1 mg/kg every 8 hours uses the synergy dose, not the once-daily 5-7 mg/kg dose used for gram-negative sepsis; trough should be below 1 mg/L within 5 days of starting.

sources.label: ESC 2023 Guidelines for the management of endocarditis

subtopic_detail: Complete hydatidiform mole - diagnosis and surveillance
stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 woman presents at an estimated 10 weeks gestation with vaginal bleeding for three days and severe nausea and vomiting that has limited oral intake to sips. She has no abdominal pain. Speculum examination shows the cervical os closed with a small amount of dark blood. The uterus is palpable to the umbilicus and feels boggy. She had a normal first pregnancy and delivery two years ago and her booking bloods are otherwise unremarkable.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 104, BP 138/86, RR 18, SpO2 98 percent room air
data_table.Examination: Uterus palpable to umbilicus, boggy, non-tender. No adnexal mass on bimanual
data_table.Investigations: Quantitative beta-hCG 460000 IU/L, TSH 0.04 mIU/L, free T4 28 pmol/L, FBC and UEC unremarkable, transvaginal ultrasound shows snowstorm appearance with multiple cystic spaces and no fetal parts, bilateral theca lutein cysts

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Misoprostol vaginally to achieve medical evacuation of the molar tissue followed by structured surveillance beta-hCG weekly for 6 weeks of follow up
options.rationale: Medical evacuation with prostaglandins risks haemorrhage and embolisation of trophoblastic tissue and is contraindicated for molar pregnancy management. (Source: RANZCOG Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound and beta-hCG in 2 weeks before any planned intervention to confirm the diagnosis with serial assessment of clinical status
options.rationale: Imaging and biochemistry are already diagnostic of complete molar pregnancy and delay risks ongoing haemorrhage, hyperthyroidism and pre-eclampsia. (Source: RANZCOG Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: Hysterectomy with bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy to remove the theca lutein cysts and definitively prevent malignancy in a young woman who has completed her family
options.rationale: Hysterectomy with bilateral oophorectomy is overtreatment that destroys fertility; theca lutein cysts regress spontaneously and oophorectomy is not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: Suction curettage under anaesthesia with histopathology and post-evacuation serial weekly serum beta-hCG until three undetectable values then monthly for 6 months
options.rationale: Suction curettage with histology and structured serial beta-hCG surveillance is the standard Australian management; persistent trophoblastic disease is detected by plateauing or rising hCG. (Source: RANZCOG Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

options.text: Single-agent intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg per square metre as primary chemotherapy and avoid all surgical evacuation in a haemodynamically stable patient today
options.rationale: Methotrexate is for confirmed gestational trophoblastic neoplasia after evacuation with rising or plateau hCG, not for primary mole management. (Source: RANZCOG Gestational Trophoblastic Disease)

explanation.summary: Complete hydatidiform mole presents with markedly elevated beta-hCG, snowstorm ultrasound, theca lutein cysts and may cause biochemical hyperthyroidism from beta-hCG cross-reactivity at the TSH receptor. Suction curettage under anaesthesia is the definitive Australian management with histological confirmation. Serial weekly beta-hCG until three consecutive undetectable values then monthly for 6 months detects gestational trophoblastic neoplasia early. Effective contraception is required throughout surveillance to avoid confounding hCG interpretation. Anti-D is given to RhD-negative women at evacuation.
key_points: Complete mole - snowstorm uterus, very high hCG, no fetal parts.
Suction curettage is the definitive treatment.
Serial hCG surveillance detects neoplasia early.
Effective contraception for 6 months protects monitoring.
Theca lutein cysts regress spontaneously.
explanation.pearls: Notify the patient that any subsequent pregnancy needs an early ultrasound at 7-8 weeks and a 6-week postnatal hCG because of the 1-2 percent risk of recurrence.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 38 Gestational Trophoblastic Disease

subtopic_detail: Croup - severity assessment and initial therapy
stem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department at 23:00 with a 24-hour history of coryza, low-grade fever and a barking cough that has become harsher overnight. He has audible inspiratory stridor at rest but is comfortable on his mother's lap. He drinks half a cup of water without difficulty and his colour is normal. There is no drooling or tripod posture. He is fully immunised.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.0, HR 132, RR 32, SpO2 97 percent room air
data_table.Examination: Stridor at rest, mild intercostal recession, no tracheal tug, alert, well perfused
data_table.Investigations: Bedside clinical assessment only - no investigations indicated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial treatment?

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 2.5 mg every 20 minutes for 3 doses and oral prednisolone 2 mg/kg in the emergency department
options.rationale: Salbutamol has no role in croup as the obstruction is supraglottic; using it delays effective steroid therapy. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 4 mg/kg and admission for observation overnight on continuous saturation monitoring
options.rationale: Intravenous steroids are unnecessary because oral dexamethasone is rapidly absorbed and equally effective without cannulation distress. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg single dose and observation for until stridor at rest settles (typically 1-2 hours) with discharge if stridor settles at rest
options.rationale: A single oral dose of dexamethasone is the standard Australian first-line treatment for mild to moderate croup with rapid onset of effect and durable benefit. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Nebulised adrenaline 5 mL of 1 in 1000 immediately as the first intervention before any other treatment
options.rationale: Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe croup with marked stridor at rest, retractions and distress; it is not first-line for mild stridor. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

options.text: Discharge home with advice and saline nasal drops alone given the child is feeding and is well perfused at presentation
options.rationale: Discharging without dexamethasone misses the opportunity to shorten symptoms and reduce return rates, even when the child is feeding well. (Source: RCH CPG Croup)

explanation.summary: Croup is a viral laryngotracheitis with subglottic oedema causing barking cough and inspiratory stridor. Severity is graded by stridor at rest, retractions, oxygenation and mental status. A single dose of oral dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg shortens duration and prevents readmission across the mild to moderate spectrum. Nebulised adrenaline is reserved for severe croup with significant respiratory distress; its effect lasts about 2 hours so observation for at least 4 hours after adrenaline is required. Discharge is safe if the child is stridor-free at rest, well hydrated and the parent can return easily.
key_points: Single oral dexamethasone for all mild and moderate croup.
Nebulised adrenaline for severe croup only.
Observe at least 4 hours after adrenaline before discharge.
No role for salbutamol in croup.
Avoid throat examination and IV access if not needed.
explanation.pearls: Choose dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg oral over prednisolone 1 mg/kg even though both are used; dexamethasone has a longer half-life and is single-dose, reducing risk of bounce-back stridor.

stem: A 28-year-old man presents with 7 days of fever, sore throat, generalised myalgia, headache and a maculopapular rash on his torso. He returned from Bali 4 weeks ago. He reports condomless receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner 3 weeks ago. He is on no medications and has no allergies. Examination shows pharyngitis without exudate, fine truncal rash and a few small palpable cervical lymph nodes. There is no meningism.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.6, HR 102, BP 116/72, RR 16, SpO2 98 percent room air
data_table.Examination: Pharyngitis, maculopapular truncal rash, soft cervical lymphadenopathy, no meningism, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: WCC 4.1, lymphocytes 0.9, platelets 118, ALT 78, CRP 32, monospot negative

lead_in: Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm acute HIV infection at this stage?

options.text: Fourth-generation HIV antigen-antibody combination immunoassay together with quantitative HIV-1 RNA viral load
options.rationale: Fourth-generation combination assay detects p24 antigen and antibody, and quantitative HIV-1 RNA viral load detects acute infection during the antibody window. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: HIV-1 antibody Western blot alone, since the combination assay is unreliable in the acute window before antibody seroconversion
options.rationale: Western blot is a confirmatory antibody-only test that is negative or indeterminate during acute infection before antibody is detectable. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Single CD4 count and CD4 to CD8 ratio measurement, which establishes the diagnosis when the CD4 count is below 500 cells per microlitre
options.rationale: CD4 count quantifies immune status but does not diagnose HIV; lymphopenia can occur in many acute viral infections. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: EBV serology with monospot and acute and convalescent IgM and IgG titres to exclude infectious mononucleosis before testing for HIV
options.rationale: Treating EBV as a competing diagnosis delays the time-critical diagnosis of acute HIV when post-exposure transmission risk is at its peak. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: A repeat HIV combination immunoassay in 12 weeks and presumptive empirical treatment with valaciclovir for suspected primary HSV infection
options.rationale: Delaying repeat testing 12 weeks misses the opportunity to confirm acute HIV and initiate early antiretroviral therapy that reduces viral reservoir. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents as a mononucleosis-like illness with fever, pharyngitis, rash, lymphadenopathy, and transient cytopenias 2-4 weeks after exposure. Fourth-generation combination immunoassay detects both p24 antigen and HIV antibodies and shortens the window to about 2 weeks. Quantitative HIV-1 RNA confirms infection before antibodies develop and viral loads in acute infection are typically very high. Early ART improves outcomes and reduces forward transmission. Australian framework requires partner notification, contact tracing and consideration of post-exposure prophylaxis for recent contacts.
key_points: Fever, pharyngitis, rash, lymphadenopathy after exposure - test for acute HIV.
Fourth-generation combination assay plus HIV RNA detect acute infection.
Initiate ART promptly to reduce reservoir and transmission.
Counsel about partner notification and confidentiality.
Offer post-exposure prophylaxis to contacts within 72 hours of exposure.
explanation.pearls: If HIV combination assay is positive but RNA undetectable, suspect an elite controller or false-positive serology; repeat both assays from a fresh sample and refer urgently to a HIV s100 prescriber.

stem: A 27-year-old G1 woman attends for her 12-week dating scan after a spontaneous conception. She is excited to learn she has a twin pregnancy. She is otherwise well with no significant medical history and has been taking pre-pregnancy folic acid. The sonographer notes two distinct fetal poles each with normal early structures and clear cardiac activity. She asks how the type of twins will affect her care plan.

data_table.Examination: Uterus palpable just above pubic symphysis
data_table.Investigations: Crown-rump length consistent with 12+1 weeks both twins. Single placental mass, T-sign at membrane-placental junction, thin intertwin membrane
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin with iodine and folic acid

lead_in: Which feature on this scan most reliably establishes chorionicity?

options.text: Crown-rump length concordance between the two fetuses regardless of placental morphology or membrane appearance
options.rationale: Crown-rump length concordance reassures about size but does not determine chorionicity. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Number of yolk sacs visible on first trimester ultrasound is the gold standard for chorionicity assignment after 12 weeks
options.rationale: Yolk sac number helps assess amnionicity in early pregnancy but is no longer reliable after 10 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Two separate placental discs on second trimester anatomy scan confirm dichorionic pregnancy more reliably than first trimester signs
options.rationale: Two separate placentas confirm dichorionic but a single placental mass does not exclude dichorionic; first trimester signs are most accurate. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Fetal sex discordance on cell-free fetal DNA testing is the only reliable non-ultrasound method for chorionicity determination
options.rationale: Discordant fetal sex confirms dichorionic but concordant sex does not exclude dichorionic; ultrasound signs remain primary. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: T-sign at the membrane-placental junction with a thin intertwin membrane indicates monochorionic-diamniotic pregnancy
options.rationale: The T-sign with a thin two-layer intertwin membrane reliably identifies monochorionic-diamniotic placentation when assessed before 14 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Chorionicity is the single most important determinant of risk and surveillance in twin pregnancy. The T-sign with a thin two-layer membrane indicates monochorionic-diamniotic placentation; the lambda or twin-peak sign indicates dichorionic. Monochorionic twins need fortnightly ultrasound from 16 weeks for twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome surveillance and selective fetal growth restriction. Dichorionic twins need 4-weekly ultrasound from 24 weeks. First trimester is the optimal window for chorionicity assessment because signs become less reliable later in pregnancy.
key_points: T-sign equals monochorionic, lambda-sign equals dichorionic.
Determine chorionicity in the first trimester.
Monochorionic twins need fortnightly surveillance from 16 weeks.
Dichorionic twins need 4-weekly surveillance from 24 weeks.
Refer monochorionic twins early to maternal-fetal medicine.
explanation.pearls: If chorionicity is unclear or assessment is delayed past 14 weeks, treat as monochorionic for the more intensive surveillance schedule rather than risk under-monitoring.

sources.label: ISUOG Practice Guidelines Role of Ultrasound in Twin Pregnancy

stem: A 34-year-old woman from Vietnam who immigrated to Australia 6 years ago is being assessed before commencing infliximab for inflammatory bowel disease. She has no respiratory symptoms, no contact with anyone with tuberculosis and a normal chest x-ray. She received BCG vaccination in childhood. She is asymptomatic with normal observations.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7, HR 72, BP 118/70, SpO2 99 percent room air
data_table.Investigations: Chest x-ray reported as normal, QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus positive with both TB1 and TB2 antigen tubes above cut-off, sputum unable to be obtained
data_table.Medications: Mesalazine, prednisolone 10 mg daily, planned infliximab

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence infliximab immediately because the positive QuantiFERON likely represents BCG vaccination from childhood, not true latent infection
options.rationale: QuantiFERON Gold Plus antigens do not cross-react with BCG vaccination so a positive result indicates Mycobacterium tuberculosis exposure. (Source: NTAC Latent TB Infection Guideline)

options.text: Begin a 9-month course of isoniazid 5 mg/kg daily with monthly liver function tests and proceed with infliximab in parallel without further evaluation
options.rationale: Active TB must be excluded before treating as latent, otherwise monotherapy risks resistance and progression on biologic therapy. (Source: NTAC Latent TB Infection Guideline)

options.text: Repeat QuantiFERON in 12 weeks to confirm persistent positivity before deciding whether to delay biologic initiation in a symptom-free patient
options.rationale: Repeating QuantiFERON without exclusion of active disease and treatment delays both immunosuppression and tuberculosis management. (Source: NTAC Latent TB Infection Guideline)

options.text: Confirm absence of active tuberculosis with sputum induction and CT chest, then treat latent TB with isoniazid plus pyridoxine for 9 months before commencing infliximab
options.rationale: Active TB must first be excluded with directed imaging and microbiology; once excluded, latent TB treatment is given before TNF-alpha inhibitor therapy. (Source: NTAC Latent TB Infection Guideline)

options.text: Refer for directly observed RIPE four-drug therapy as latent TB treatment standard before biologic initiation in this high-risk patient population
options.rationale: RIPE four-drug therapy is for active tuberculosis disease; latent TB is treated with isoniazid monotherapy or short rifamycin regimens. (Source: NTAC Latent TB Infection Guideline)

explanation.summary: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus uses TB-specific antigens that do not cross-react with BCG vaccination, so a positive result in this patient indicates Mycobacterium tuberculosis infection. Active TB must be excluded by clinical assessment, chest imaging and respiratory samples before latent treatment to avoid generating resistance. Standard latent TB treatment options include isoniazid 9 months with pyridoxine, rifampicin 4 months or weekly rifapentine plus isoniazid 12 doses. TNF-alpha inhibitors substantially increase risk of reactivation, so treatment should start before biologic initiation when possible. Monitor for hepatotoxicity with monthly liver function tests.
key_points: QuantiFERON-Plus does not cross-react with BCG.
Exclude active TB before treating latent TB.
Isoniazid 9 months with pyridoxine is standard latent therapy.
Start latent TB treatment before TNF-alpha inhibitor.
Monitor LFTs and adherence monthly.
explanation.pearls: Rifapentine plus isoniazid weekly for 12 doses (3HP regimen) is now preferred in many jurisdictions for adherence; rifapentine is not currently widely available in Australia but is on the NTAC option list.

stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 woman is being managed in the second stage of labour at 40+2 weeks. Her previous baby was 4.1 kg without complications. After delivery of the head, the head retracts tightly against the perineum, the chin does not turn freely and the anterior shoulder fails to deliver with routine axial traction. The bedside midwife immediately calls for help and the obstetric registrar is in the room. The clock is started.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 124/80, HR 96, fetal HR last recorded 130 bpm 30 seconds ago
data_table.Examination: Turtle sign present, fetal head retracted against perineum, chin not rotating
data_table.Investigations: Estimated fetal weight at 39 weeks 4.4 kg, gestational diabetes diet-controlled

lead_in: After calling for help and stopping maternal pushing, what is the next most appropriate manoeuvre?

options.text: Apply firm fundal pressure with downward traction on the fetal head to disimpact the anterior shoulder beneath the symphysis pubis
options.rationale: Fundal pressure worsens shoulder impaction against the symphysis and increases brachial plexus injury risk. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform McRoberts manoeuvre with hyperflexion of the maternal hips against the abdomen plus suprapubic pressure from above the symphysis
options.rationale: McRoberts hyperflexion plus suprapubic pressure increases the functional pelvic outlet and resolves about 60-70 percent of shoulder dystocia cases as the first manoeuvre. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Proceed directly to symphysiotomy with local anaesthesia to widen the pelvic outlet and free the anterior shoulder for immediate delivery
options.rationale: Symphysiotomy is a last-resort manoeuvre after multiple internal manoeuvres have failed and carries significant maternal morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Perform Zavanelli manoeuvre with cephalic replacement and immediate transfer to theatre for emergency category 1 caesarean delivery
options.rationale: Zavanelli is reserved for failed all-out manoeuvres and carries very high fetal mortality; it is not the first manoeuvre. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

options.text: Deliberately fracture the fetal clavicle with thumb pressure to reduce shoulder diameter and facilitate immediate vaginal delivery
options.rationale: Deliberate clavicular fracture is a salvage procedure after internal rotational manoeuvres and is rarely the first intervention. (Source: RANZCOG Shoulder Dystocia)

explanation.summary: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency where additional manoeuvres beyond gentle axial traction are required to deliver the shoulders. The HELPERR mnemonic structures the response: help, evaluate for episiotomy, legs in McRoberts, pressure suprapubically, enter for internal rotation, remove posterior arm, and roll onto all fours. McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure resolves the majority within 30 seconds; fundal pressure is contraindicated. If unsuccessful, escalate to internal rotational manoeuvres such as Rubin and Woods screw, then delivery of the posterior arm, then all-fours position. Document time intervals, manoeuvres used and cord gases.
key_points: Call for help and start the clock immediately.
McRoberts plus suprapubic pressure first - resolves most cases.
Avoid fundal pressure - worsens impaction.
Escalate through internal rotation and posterior arm delivery.
Document time, manoeuvres, cord gases and brachial plexus assessment.
explanation.pearls: Pre-delivery risk factors for shoulder dystocia (prior dystocia, fetal weight above 4.5 kg in non-diabetic or above 4 kg in diabetic) should be discussed antenatally; planned caesarean is offered when estimated fetal weight exceeds 4.5 kg in pregnancies complicated by diabetes.

subtopic_detail: Necrotising soft tissue infection - recognition and management
stem: A 54-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with 36 hours of severe progressively worsening left lower leg pain following a minor garden injury. The pain is out of proportion to the modest erythema seen at the wound. There is dusky discolouration over a 10 cm area with one tense fluid-filled bulla. He is febrile with tachycardia and feels unwell. He has had paracetamol with no relief.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9, HR 124, BP 96/58, RR 26, SpO2 96 percent room air
data_table.Examination: Lower limb erythema, dusky 10 cm area, tense haemorrhagic bulla, crepitus to palpation, exquisite pain on light touch
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22, neutrophils 19, Na 128, creatinine 142, glucose 16, CRP 320, lactate 4.6, CK 8400, hb 132

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Plain x-ray of the left lower limb followed by venous duplex ultrasound to exclude deep vein thrombosis prior to antibiotic selection
options.rationale: Imaging delays surgical intervention; necrotising fasciitis is a clinical diagnosis with definitive treatment in theatre. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Admit for intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g every 6 hours and reassess in 24 hours if symptoms have not improved before considering surgical referral
options.rationale: Antibiotic monotherapy without urgent surgical exploration in suspected necrotising fasciitis significantly increases mortality. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Immediate fluid resuscitation, intravenous meropenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin, and emergent surgical debridement without delay for imaging
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis demands immediate broad spectrum antibiotics with clindamycin for toxin suppression and urgent surgical debridement; imaging must not delay theatre. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Compute LRINEC score using laboratory values and observe in resuscitation area for 6 hours before deciding on surgical exploration if score is below 6
options.rationale: LRINEC supports but does not replace clinical judgement; a low score does not exclude necrotising fasciitis with classic clinical features. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

options.text: Apply topical mupirocin to the wound, elevate the limb and arrange outpatient surgical review for early necrotising fasciitis with negative blood cultures
options.rationale: Outpatient management is inappropriate; necrotising fasciitis requires inpatient resuscitation, IV antibiotics and theatre. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection)
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency with mortality exceeding 25 percent and rising rapidly with delay. Pain out of proportion to skin findings, systemic toxicity, crepitus, dusky discolouration and haemorrhagic bullae are red flags. Broad spectrum cover with carbapenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin (for toxin suppression in group A streptococcal disease) is started immediately, with surgical debridement within 6 hours as the strongest determinant of survival. The LRINEC score supports but never excludes the diagnosis when clinical features are present. Repeat debridement and ICU admission are usually required.
key_points: Pain out of proportion plus crepitus or bullae - necrotising fasciitis.
Imaging must not delay surgery in clear clinical cases.
Carbapenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin empirically.
Surgical debridement within 6 hours saves lives.
LRINEC supports but does not exclude the diagnosis.
explanation.pearls: Add clindamycin specifically for ribosomal toxin suppression in group A streptococcal infection; it works even when beta-lactam efficacy is reduced by high bacterial load in the Eagle effect.

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic - Skin and Soft Tissue Infection
WSACS Guidelines for the Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections

stem: A 7-year-old boy is reviewed in clinic 2 weeks after diagnosis of IgA vasculitis. He presented with a palpable purpuric rash on his buttocks and lower limbs, periarticular swelling of both ankles and intermittent colicky abdominal pain. The rash and joint swelling have resolved and he is back at school. His mother has been doing weekly urinalysis as advised. The latest urinalysis dipstick today shows 2+ blood and 1+ protein.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.6, HR 92, BP 122/78 on a 130 cm 25 kg boy (above 95th centile)
data_table.Examination: No rash, no joint swelling, abdomen soft and non-tender, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis 2+ blood 1+ protein, urine ACR 80 mg/mmol (normal <2.5), serum creatinine 58, eGFR 88, albumin 36

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Reassure and continue weekly home urinalysis only, expecting spontaneous resolution within the next 4 weeks because the rash has cleared
options.rationale: Significant proteinuria and hypertension above 95th centile require specialist review and further investigation rather than continued observation. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 14 days then taper, planning empirical immunosuppression for nephritis without specialist input first
options.rationale: Empirical steroid therapy without paediatric nephrology assessment and consideration of biopsy is not standard initial management. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Stop further urinalysis monitoring and discharge from clinic because the active rash and arthritis have resolved, with return advice for abdominal pain or new rash
options.rationale: Significant proteinuria and elevated BP indicate renal involvement that requires ongoing nephrology surveillance, not discharge. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric nephrology with paired urine albumin to creatinine ratio, blood pressure confirmation and review for possible renal biopsy and treatment
options.rationale: Persistent significant proteinuria with hypertension warrants urgent paediatric nephrology referral for biopsy consideration and treatment to preserve kidney function. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

options.text: Commence enalapril for blood pressure control and arrange routine outpatient nephrology review in 6 to 8 weeks for further monitoring
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are considered after nephrology assessment; delaying specialist review with significant proteinuria risks irreversible kidney damage. (Source: RCH CPG Henoch-Schonlein Purpura)

explanation.summary: IgA vasculitis (formerly Henoch-Schonlein purpura) is a small vessel vasculitis of childhood with palpable purpura, arthritis, abdominal pain and IgA glomerulonephritis. Renal involvement develops in about a third of cases, mostly within 4 to 6 weeks. Weekly home urinalysis is recommended for the first 4 weeks then less frequently for 6-12 months. Significant proteinuria (urine ACR above 30), nephrotic syndrome, hypertension or rising creatinine demand urgent nephrology referral for renal biopsy and treatment. Steroids do not prevent nephritis but reduce abdominal pain and arthritis duration in severe cases.
key_points: Monitor urinalysis weekly for 4 weeks then less frequently 6-12 months.
Refer if proteinuria ACR above 30, BP above 95th centile, or rising creatinine.
Nephritis can develop after rash and arthritis resolve.
Steroids reduce extra-renal symptoms but do not prevent nephritis.
Long-term renal follow-up needed for any nephritis.
explanation.pearls: Confirm hypertension with three separate readings using the correct paediatric cuff size and using the 95th centile threshold for age, height and sex from the AAP nomogram, not adult thresholds.

stem: A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department from his nursing home with 6 hours of fever, confusion and reduced urine output. He is normally independent in basic care and lives in the community. He has a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes and benign prostatic hypertrophy. On arrival he is drowsy and disoriented in time. The triage nurse has applied oxygen and obtained intravenous access.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.7, HR 124, BP 84/52, RR 28, SpO2 92 percent on 2 L nasal prongs
data_table.Examination: Drowsy, cool peripheries, capillary refill 4 seconds, abdomen soft, suprapubic tenderness, no meningism
data_table.Investigations: Bedside lactate 4.2, glucose 14, urinalysis nitrites and leucocytes positive, ECG sinus tachycardia, qSOFA 3

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step within the first hour of resuscitation?

options.text: Send blood cultures and obtain CT abdomen and pelvis before starting antibiotics to identify the urinary source and exclude obstruction first
options.rationale: Imaging must not delay antibiotic administration; first-dose antibiotic within an hour is the strongest hour 1 priority. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021)

options.text: Administer oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg single dose while awaiting intravenous access then transfer to a ward bed for ongoing care
options.rationale: Septic shock requires intravenous broad spectrum antibiotics; oral therapy is inadequate and admission requires high-acuity area not a ward. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021)

options.text: Draw blood cultures, give intravenous gentamicin plus piperacillin-tazobactam within 1 hour and start 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid resuscitation guided by clinical response
options.rationale: Cultures before antibiotics if achievable without delay, antibiotic within 1 hour and 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus for hypotension or lactate above 4 are the hour 1 bundle priorities. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021)

options.text: Defer all antibiotic therapy until cultures return positive in 24 to 48 hours to ensure susceptibility-targeted treatment with the narrowest agent
options.rationale: Antibiotic delay in septic shock dramatically increases mortality with about 8 percent rise for each hour of delay. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021)

options.text: Commence noradrenaline infusion via peripheral cannula at 0.05 microgram/kg/min before any intravenous fluid resuscitation is given to maintain organ perfusion
options.rationale: Vasopressors are added after initial fluid resuscitation if MAP remains below 65 mmHg, not as first-line ahead of fluids. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021)

explanation.summary: Septic shock is defined by hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP at least 65 mmHg plus lactate above 2 mmol/L despite fluid resuscitation. The hour 1 bundle covers lactate measurement, blood cultures before antibiotics if no delay, broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics within an hour, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate at least 4, and vasopressors if hypotension persists. Source control (urinary obstruction relief, abscess drainage) is essential and should occur within 6-12 hours. Re-measure lactate and reassess perfusion within 2-4 hours.
key_points: Hour 1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors as needed.
Antibiotics within 1 hour - each hour of delay increases mortality.
30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate at least 4.
Noradrenaline as first-line vasopressor when MAP below 65 after fluids.
Source control within 6-12 hours of recognition.
explanation.pearls: Use balanced crystalloid (Hartmann's or Plasma-Lyte) over 0.9 percent sodium chloride because chloride-restrictive strategies show reduced AKI in critically ill patients (SMART and PLUS trials).
